title: Saturn–Neptune Conjunction
visible_in: Best seen after dusk where Saturn is up; Neptune is very faint and requires binoculars or a telescope. Avoid searching too close to the Sun.
description: Saturn and Neptune appear close together in the sky. Saturn is easy to see, but Neptune is very faint. Use binoculars/telescope to confirm Neptune. A clear, darker sky helps.

title: Crescent Moon near Mercury
visible_in: Low in bright twilight—best with a clear western horizon. Easier at lower latitudes where the ecliptic can be steeper.
description: A thin crescent Moon appears near Mercury low in evening twilight. You’ll need a very clear horizon and good timing after sunset. Binoculars may help, but never sweep near the Sun.

title: Moon close to Saturn
visible_in: Visible from most places with a clear evening view; best once twilight deepens.
description: The crescent Moon passes close to Saturn, making an easy and striking pairing. Both are visible to the naked eye. Binoculars add detail and make the scene more dramatic.

title: Venus–Mercury Conjunction (Low Twilight)
visible_in: Very low and close to the Sun—visibility depends strongly on horizon and latitude. Best shortly after sunset with an unobstructed west.
description: Venus and Mercury appear close together but very low in twilight. Venus is brilliant; Mercury can be tricky. Use binoculars only after the Sun is safely down. A clear horizon is essential.

title: Six-Planet Morning Line-up
source: Compiled from In-The-Sky.org and major observing guides
visible_in: Visible worldwide but the layout changes by hemisphere; best before sunrise (or in late twilight). Uranus/Neptune typically need binoculars or a telescope.
description: Several planets gather along the ecliptic in a short window. Bright planets are easy, but Uranus and Neptune are faint. Expect low-horizon targets—choose a dark site with clear skies and a wide view.

title: Venus–Neptune Close Conjunction
visible_in: Twilight-challenging and close to the Sun. Best attempted only when the Sun is fully down and with a very clear horizon; Neptune requires optics.
description: Venus passes extremely close to faint Neptune. Venus is obvious, but Neptune is very dim and needs binoculars/telescope. This pairing is difficult because it’s near solar glare—use extra care and never point optics near the Sun.

title: Venus–Saturn Conjunction
visible_in: Low after sunset—best with a clear western horizon. Easier from locations with a steep ecliptic and good transparency.
description: Bright Venus and golden Saturn appear close together, but often low in twilight. Both are naked-eye planets; binoculars help in glare. Wait until the Sun is fully down before scanning low horizons.

title: Mars–Mercury Conjunction
visible_in: Very close to the Sun for many locations—often hard or impossible to observe. If visible, it will be low in twilight with a clear horizon.
description: Mars and Mercury appear near each other but typically buried in bright twilight. Mercury can be brief and low. Only attempt after sunset (or before sunrise) and never sweep binoculars near the Sun.

title: Mars–Neptune Conjunction
visible_in: Neptune needs binoculars/telescope. Often low or twilight-limited depending on location—best from darker skies and a clear horizon.
description: Mars passes close to Neptune, making Neptune easier to locate relative to a bright planet. Neptune is very faint and needs optics. Expect twilight or low-altitude challenges for many observers.

title: Mercury–Neptune Conjunction
visible_in: Very challenging: Neptune is faint and Mercury is low/twilight-limited. Best from low latitudes with a very clear horizon (still difficult).
description: Mercury meets Neptune in bright twilight. Neptune requires binoculars/telescope and dark skies, which clashes with Mercury’s low, twilight appearance. Treat this as an advanced observation challenge.

title: Mercury–Mars Conjunction
visible_in: Low and twilight-limited; best with an unobstructed horizon. Mercury is usually the limiting factor.
description: Mercury and Mars appear close together but often very low near the horizon. Mercury may be brief in twilight. Binoculars can help once the Sun is safely down (or well before sunrise).

title: Mercury–Saturn Conjunction
visible_in: Often extremely low in dawn twilight. A perfectly clear horizon is needed; many locations will not see it well.
description: Mercury passes close to Saturn, but the pair is usually buried in twilight near the horizon. Mercury can be bright, Saturn fainter. Only attempt with a clear horizon and safe timing away from the Sun.

title: Saturn–Mars Conjunction
visible_in: Can be low/twilight-dependent for many locations. Best with a clear horizon and after twilight deepens (where possible).
description: Mars and Saturn appear close together. Both are visible to the naked eye, but the real challenge is twilight and low altitude for many regions. Binoculars can help pick them out in haze.

title: Venus–Uranus Conjunction
visible_in: Uranus is faint: use binoculars/telescope, using Venus as a bright guide. Visibility depends on twilight and horizon at your location.
description: Venus pairs with Uranus, making Uranus easier to locate. Venus is brilliant; Uranus is faint and usually needs binoculars. A darker sky and good transparency improve your chances.

title: Venus–Jupiter Conjunction
visible_in: A bright, easy pairing visible from most locations after dusk (where the planets are above the horizon). Great for casual viewing and photos.
description: The two brightest planets appear close together—one of the best naked-eye planet pairings of the year. Easy to spot once the sky darkens. Binoculars make it even more impressive.

title: Mars–Uranus Ultra-Close Conjunction
visible_in: Uranus is faint: binoculars/telescope required. Best where the pair is above the horizon in darker conditions (twilight can be an issue).
description: Mars passes extremely close to Uranus—tight enough to fit in many telescope fields. Mars is easy; Uranus needs optics. This is a great ‘find Uranus’ opportunity when conditions cooperate.

title: Jupiter–Mercury Conjunction (Dawn Twilight)
visible_in: Very close to the Sun in the dawn sky—hard and horizon-dependent. Only attempt well before sunrise with a clear eastern horizon.
description: Jupiter and Mercury appear close together but usually low in bright dawn twilight. Mercury can be bright; Jupiter is steadier. Use binoculars only when the Sun is safely below the horizon.

title: Venus–Mercury Conjunction (Low Dusk)
visible_in: Often extremely low at dusk and may be hard to observe from many locations. A clear western horizon is essential.
description: Venus and Mercury pair up in the dusk sky, but the main challenge is low altitude and twilight. Venus is very bright; Mercury can be brief. Try from an elevated spot with a clean horizon.

title: Jupiter–Mars Conjunction
visible_in: Visible from many locations in the pre-dawn sky (where above the horizon). Excellent naked-eye contrast and great for binoculars.
description: Reddish Mars passes close to bright Jupiter, making a striking naked-eye pairing. The planets are bright and easy to spot when they’re up in your sky. Binoculars enhance the color contrast.

title: Crescent Moon meets Venus
visible_in: Visible from most places where Venus is up in twilight or early night. Great photo opportunity with a clear horizon.
description: A slender crescent Moon appears beside dazzling Venus. Easy to spot with the naked eye and often one of the prettiest sky pairings. Binoculars show Earthshine and a sharper crescent.

title: Jupiter–Mars Close Approach
visible_in: Widely visible at night from many locations. Not ultra-tight, but easy and bright—good for casual stargazing.
description: Jupiter and Mars make a noticeable close pairing in the evening sky. Both are bright enough for naked-eye viewing. Binoculars improve framing and bring out Mars’ color.

title: Mercury–Neptune Conjunction
visible_in: Very close to the Sun—difficult twilight event. Neptune requires optics; Mercury may be low. Attempt only with extreme care and safe Sun avoidance.
description: Mercury meets faint Neptune near solar glare. Neptune is too dim without optics, and Mercury is often low in bright twilight. Treat as an advanced challenge; do not use optics near the Sun.

title: Venus–Neptune Conjunction
visible_in: Neptune needs binoculars/telescope. Often twilight-limited depending on location—best when the pair is above the horizon in darker sky.
description: Venus passes close to Neptune, making Neptune easier to pinpoint. Neptune is faint and needs optics. A darker sky window and clear horizon improve the view.

title: Venus–Saturn Conjunction
visible_in: Low and twilight-dependent for many observers. Best with a clear horizon and after the Sun is fully down.
description: Venus and Saturn appear close together, but often low in twilight. Venus is bright; Saturn is fainter but still naked-eye. Binoculars help pull Saturn out of haze and glow.

title: Mercury–Uranus Conjunction
visible_in: Very close to the Sun—extremely difficult. Uranus requires binoculars/telescope and a dark sky, which rarely aligns with Mercury’s twilight visibility.
description: Mercury pairs with faint Uranus in bright twilight. Uranus needs optics; Mercury stays low near the Sun. Treat this as a “hard mode” event: clear horizon, safe timing, and never scan near the Sun.

title: Venus–Uranus Conjunction
visible_in: Uranus is faint and needs binoculars/telescope; Venus is the bright guide. Often close to the Sun—twilight can make it difficult.
description: Venus passes near Uranus, making Uranus easier to locate. Uranus still needs binoculars or a telescope. Because the pairing can be twilight-limited, choose a very clear horizon and a dark site if possible.

title: Venus–Mercury Conjunction (Low Dusk)
visible_in: Very low at dusk for many locations and may be difficult to observe. Best with a completely unobstructed western horizon.
description: Venus and Mercury appear near each other but often sit very low in twilight. Venus is bright; Mercury can be brief and faint. Try from a high viewpoint with clear air and safe timing after sunset.

title: Venus–Mars Close Conjunction
visible_in: Tight pairing but often low and twilight-limited depending on latitude. Best right after sunset where the planets are above the horizon.
description: Venus and Mars pass very close together, creating a striking color contrast—if your horizon and twilight timing cooperate. Venus is brilliant; Mars is dimmer but obvious nearby. Binoculars are great for framing in twilight.

title: 🚨 TEST Alignment – 21:00
visible_in: Test event
description: Push test alignment event at 21:00 UK (1h reminder should fire at 20:00 UK; 24h reminder should fire at 2026-03-04 21:00 UK).
